one_line_takeaway: SecurityLingua turns prompt compression into a jailbreak defense: a token-classifier extracts a prompt's true malicious intent and injects it into the system prompt to re-arm the LLM's built-in guardrails, cutting average attack success from 35% to 1% at ~32 extra tokens and ~25 ms - beating heavier defenses on both robustness and utility.
key_finding: SecurityLingua drives the average jailbreak attack success rate down to 1% across three models and four attack types, versus 35% with no defense and beating every baseline (PPL Filter 21%, SmoothLLM 7%, Erase-and-check 10%, IA 4%, JClassifier/JDetector 6%) - roughly 4x better than the next-best defense (Erase-and-check). It achieves this at a tiny cost: only ~32 extra tokens per query (~11% of prompt length) and ~25 ms added latency (single forward pass), compared with SmoothLLM's ~4,260 extra tokens / ~2,000 ms and Erase-and-check's ~9,000 extra tokens / ~4,200 ms (sequential). It also preserves utility: on GPT-4 it reaches an average task score of 72.0 with a 0% refusal rate, slightly ABOVE the undefended baseline (69.7), whereas SmoothLLM degrades utility to 58.0 and PPL Filter/SmoothLLM refuse ~8%/~4% of benign queries.
core_contribution: SecurityLingua, a jailbreak-defense method that repurposes prompt compression as a security pre-processing step. The authors train a security-aware token-classification compressor (an XLM-RoBERTa-style pre-trained Transformer encoder plus a linear layer, following the LLMLingua-2 framing) that discerns and extracts the 'true intention' of an input prompt, robust to the adversarial noise jailbreaks use to bury malicious intent. Unlike prior compression work (LLMLingua/LLMLingua-2) which aims only at efficiency, SecurityLingua's compressor is trained to surface malicious intent. Crucially, the compressed intention is NOT substituted for the user prompt; instead the original prompt is left fully intact and the extracted intention is injected into the SYSTEM prompt (e.g. 'The user wants you to devise a plan for selling illegal goods online'), which stimulates the target LLM's own built-in safety guardrails. This makes it plug-and-play across both proprietary and open models (prompt-only, no weight access), avoids over-defense/false-positive refusals, and adds negligible token/latency cost.
threat_model: Jailbreak attacks against safety-aligned LLMs, where an adversary wraps an original malicious instruction (e.g. how to make napalm/a bomb, sell illegal goods) inside an adversarial prompt (role-play/'grandma trick', optimization-based GCG suffixes, automated PAIR refinement, random-search suffixes) to bypass the model's safety guardrails and elicit harmful output. The defense sits between the user and the target LLM. The paper evaluates against four JailbreakBench attack families (PAIR, GCG, JB-Chat, RS) but does NOT evaluate adaptive attacks aimed at the compressor itself.
limitations: Reviewer-inferred (the paper has no dedicated limitations section): (1) The defense is not self-sufficient - it works by re-triggering the target LLM's OWN guardrails, so on a model with weak or no safety alignment, highlighting the intention may not induce refusal. (2) Evaluation is confined to JailbreakBench's four attack families; the RS (random-search) attack remains the hardest case even for SecurityLingua (5% success on Llama2-7B and GPT-3.5). (3) No adaptive/white-box attack against the compressor is tested - an attacker aware of the intention-extraction step could craft prompts to evade or poison it. (4) Training labels are produced by LLMs (GPT-4o/Mistral/Uncensored-LLaMA) via a heuristic 'sorry/cannot' rejection check and fuzzy alignment, so annotation noise and model bias can propagate into the compressor. (5) Utility is measured only on four multiple-choice/QA-style benchmarks and only refusal+accuracy, not open-ended generation quality. (6) Injecting an extracted 'intention' into the system prompt is itself an attack surface (potential for the compressor to mis-summarize benign prompts as malicious).

datasets_used: JailbreakBench (Chao et al. 2024) - defense evaluation with PAIR, GCG, JB-Chat, and RS (prompt+random-search) attacks
ARC-Hard, GPQA, MMLU, GSM8K - utility/refusal evaluation

Training corpus (221K paired examples total, avg length 262 tokens, avg compression ratio 0.72): benign - Alpaca (10K), OpenHermes (30K), Disaster-tweet (1.5K), WildJailbreak-benign (81K); malicious - JailbreakV-28K (16K), JailbreakHub (1.5K), Disaster-tweet-malicious (1.5K), WildJailbreak-malicious (80K)
quantitative_results: Average jailbreak attack success rate: SecurityLingua 1% vs None 35%, PPL Filter 21%, SmoothLLM 7%, Erase-and-check 10%, IA 4%, JClassifier 6% (Table 3, lower is better; ~4x better than next-best Erase-and-check)
Undefended peak vulnerability: 93% success (GPT-3.5 under RS attack); SecurityLingua's worst cell is 5% (RS on Llama2-7B and GPT-3.5)

Efficiency: SecurityLingua adds ~32 extra tokens (~11% of prompt) and ~25 ms latency vs SmoothLLM 4,260 tokens / 2,000 ms (500 ms batched) and Erase-and-check ~9,000 tokens / 4,200 ms (880 ms batched)

GPT-4 utility (Table 4): SecurityLingua avg score 72.0 at 0% refusal (None 69.7; IA 71.1/0%; SmoothLLM 58.0/4.1% refusal; PPL Filter 69.5/8.3% refusal); per-task Acc SecurityLingua ARC 95.0, GPQA 46.7, MMLU 88.9, GSM8K 57.5

Data pipeline: GPT-4o rejected 73.7% of malicious queries; only 0.7% required the Uncensored-LLaMA-72B fallback; QC filters drop top 5% by Variation Rate and top 10% by Alignment Gap; keep threshold tau=0.5; encoder max length 512 tokens
baselines_compared: JClassifier / JDetector (RoBERTa-based jailbreak detector inspired by constitutional classifiers, Sharma et al. 2025)
